mnemonics.title: mkdir -p Remembering the Parents Flag
mnemonics.content: Think of -p as 'Create Parents': Without -p, mkdir fails if parent doesn't exist. The -p flag makes mkdir create all parent directories automatically, just like a family tree - it creates the parents before the children!

mnemonics.title: Permission Numbers Made Easy
mnemonics.content: Remember permissions as '4-2-1': Read=4, Write=2, Execute=1. Add them up: 7=rwx (4+2+1), 5=r-x (4+1), 4=r--. For 755: Owner gets 7, Group gets 5, Others get 5.

mnemonics.title: Verbose Flag Visualization
mnemonics.content: Think of -v as 'Verbal' - it talks to you! Just like a verbose person tells you everything they do, -v tells you every directory it creates.

acronyms.usage: The command name itself is an acronym representing its function - creating directories in the file system.
Creates parent directories as needed when creating nested directory structures.
Sets the permission mode (like 755, 644) for the newly created directory.
Shows detailed output - prints a message for each directory created.
Three permission types: r=view content, w=modify content, x=enter/access directory.

visualTips.visualization: Think of mkdir like building a house: mkdir house creates the house. mkdir -p floors/rooms/kitchen creates floors first, then rooms, then kitchen - automatically building the complete structure!
Picture a 3x3 grid: Owner | Group | Others × Read | Write | Execute. Each cell gets 1 (allowed) or 0 (denied). 755 fills as: Owner=111, Group=101, Others=101.
Visualize directory paths as a tree: /home/user/projects is a tree trunk (home) with branches (user, projects). mkdir -p plants the entire tree; mkdir only works if the trunk exists.

analogies.analogy: Think of mkdir like creating a new folder in a file cabinet. Just as you'd label and place a new folder in the cabinet, mkdir creates and labels a new directory in the file system.
Creating /home/user/docs is like adding a great-grandchild to a family tree. The -p flag ensures all ancestors (parents, grandparents) exist before adding the child.
Directory permissions are like house keys: 7 (rwx) gives owner full key to enter, modify, and use the room. 5 (r-x) gives others a copy of the key to enter and look but not change anything.
